subject: Chemistry
Chemistry
solution:
When metal has low oxidation state, it has more electron density in d orbital. So it has more tendency to back donate electrons. Thus compound must have good π acceptor ligand.

 π backbonding, also called π backdonation, is when electrons move from an atomic orbital on one atom to an appropriate symmetry antibonding orbital on a π-acceptor ligand.It is especially common in the organometallic chemistry of transition metals with multi-atomic ligands such as carbon monoxide, ethylene or the nitrosonium cation. Electrons from the metal are used to bond to the ligand, in the process relieving the metal of excess negative charge. Compounds where π backbonding occurs include Ni(CO)4 and Zeise's salt.

The metal to ligand bonding creates a synergic effect which strengthens the bond between ligand and the metal

subject: Chemistry
solution:
(A) $\left[\mathrm{FeO}_4\right]^{2-} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{6+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^2$
(B) $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_8\right]^{3-} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^5$
(C) $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_5 \mathrm{NO}\right]^{2-} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^6$
(D) $[\mathrm{CoCl} 4]^{2-} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Co}^{2+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^7$
(E) $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_3 \mathrm{~F}_3\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{Co}^{3+}=3 \mathrm{~d}^6$
(B) and (D) are homoleptic complex having odd no. of d electrons.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Number of covalent bond formed by Boron is 4.

BF4- Covalency =4 

The oxidation state of an element represents the charge it would have if all the bonding electrons were assigned to the more electronegative atom in the bond.

Oxidation number of fluorine is -1

Then,

B + 4 x (-1) = -1B-4=-1B=+3

Oxidation state =+3 for Boron

solution:
On increasing strength of ligand, wavelength of absorbed radiation decreases
Strength of ligands according to spectrochemical series is: NH3>H2O>Cl-
∴  Order of wavelength absorbed will be Cl->H2O>NH3
∴  Correct order of wavelength absorbed is
I>II>III

subject: Chemistry
solution: Cis - Platin PtNH32Cl2 is a chemotherapy medication used to treat a number of cancers. It is given by injection into a vein. Cisplatin binds to the N7 reactive center on purine residues and as such can cause deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) damage in cancer cells, blocking cell division and resulting in apoptotic cell death.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Here en = ethylene diamine 

 

 [PtCl2(en)2]2+ forms cis and trans-isomers. Only the cis-isomers has no element of symmetry. So it is optically active. Trans - [PtCl2(en)2]2+ form is optically inactive. Square planar complexes doesn't exhibit optical isomerism.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Pt(en)2Cl22+ is a square planar complex forms geometrical isomers (cis and trans). Trans isomer does not show optical isomerism since it is symmetrical, while cis isomer shows optical isomerism as it is unsymmetrical.

POS = plane of symmetry

COS = centre of symmetry

The compounds,  cis−[Co(en)2Cl2]+ and [Co(en)3]3+ show optical isomerism as they do not possess any element of symmetry.

cis-Cr(ox)2Cl23-→can show optical isomerism (no POS & COS)

trans-Pt(en)2Cl22+→can't show optical isomerism (contain POS & COS)

trans-Cr(ox)2Cl23-→can't show optical isomerism(contain POS & COS)

Hence, among the given complexes 4 complexes can show optical isomerism.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The coordination compound K3[Co(CN)6]contains a cobalt(III) ion, which has a d6 electronic configuration. The cyanide ligands in this complex are indeed strong field ligands, which means they will cause a large splitting of the d orbitals. So here cobalt is in +3 state having 3d6 configuration and also cyanide ligand which is a strong field ligand so, it is most stable and will have octahedral shape. The given compounds have the following nature:

(1) Na3[CoCl6]  – Paramagnetic

(2) NiNH36Cl2 – Paramagnetic

(3) K3Co(CN)6– Diamagnetic

(4) CoH2O6Cl2 – Paramagnetic

subject: Chemistry
options: Magnetic properties of transition metal complexes
solution: The crystal field model is successful in explaining the formation, structures, colour and magnetic properties of coordination compounds to a large extent. However, from the assumptions that the ligands are point charges, it follows that anionic ligands should exert the greatest splitting effect. The anionic ligands actually are found at the low end of the spectrochemical series. So, it couldn't explain successfully the order of spectrochemical series.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Spin only magnetic moment can be calculated by the following formula
Magnetic moment =n(n+2)
Where, n is number of unpaired electrons.

In the given compound, Pt2+d8

Pt2+→dsp2 hybridization and have no unpaired electrons..

∴ Magnetic moment

 =n(n+2)=0(0+2)=0

subject: Chemistry
solution:
BrF5 is an interhalogen compound, meaning that the molecules of this compound contain two or more different halogen atoms but no atoms of the elements belonging to any other group.

The hybridisation of bromine pentafluoride forms an sp3d2 hybridisation, resulting in a square pyramidal molecular structure. The hybrid molecule has five sigma bonds due to the covalent electron sharing.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The spin-only magnetic moment is given by $\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)}$ BM, where $n$ is the number of unpaired electrons.
For $\mu > 3.0$ BM, $n$ must be $\ge 3$ (since for $n=2$, $\mu = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83$ BM and for $n=3$, $\mu = \sqrt{15} \approx 3.87$ BM).
Electronic configurations and unpaired electrons ($n$) in high spin octahedral complexes:
Metal IonConfiguration$n$$\mu$ (BM)
$Co^{2+}$$[Ar] 3d^7$33.87
$Ni^{2+}$$[Ar] 3d^8$22.83
$Fe^{2+}$$[Ar] 3d^6$44.90
$V^{3+}$$[Ar] 3d^2$22.83
$Ti^{2+}$$[Ar] 3d^2$22.83

The metal ions with $\mu > 3.0$ BM are $Co^{2+}$ ($n=3$) and $Fe^{2+}$ ($n=4$).
The sum of unpaired electrons for these ions is $3 + 4 = 7$.

subject: Chemistry
Chemistry
solution:
To determine the magnetic nature of the given complexes, we analyze the oxidation state, electronic configuration, and the nature of the ligands for each central metal atom/ion:

1. $[\text{MnBr}_4]^{2-}$: Mn$^{2+}$ has a $3d^5$ configuration. Br$^-$ is a weak field ligand. The complex is tetrahedral with $5$ unpaired electrons. (Paramagnetic)

2. $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$: Ni$^{2+}$ has a $3d^8$ configuration. Cl$^-$ is a weak field ligand. The complex is tetrahedral with $2$ unpaired electrons. (Paramagnetic)

3. $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$: Ni$^{2+}$ has a $3d^8$ configuration. CN$^-$ is a strong field ligand. The complex is square planar ($dsp^2$) with all electrons paired. (Diamagnetic)

4. $[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]$: Ni$^0$ has a $3d^8 4s^2$ configuration, which becomes $3d^{10}$ in the presence of the strong field ligand CO. The complex is tetrahedral with all electrons paired. (Diamagnetic)

5. $[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}$: Co$^{3+}$ has a $3d^6$ configuration. F$^-$ is a weak field ligand. The complex is octahedral and high spin ($t_{2g}^4 e_g^2$) with $4$ unpaired electrons. (Paramagnetic)

6. $[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{4-}$: Fe$^{2+}$ has a $3d^6$ configuration. CN$^-$ is a strong field ligand. The complex is octahedral and low spin ($t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$) with all electrons paired. (Diamagnetic)

7. $[\text{Mn(CN)}_6]^{3-}$: Mn$^{3+}$ has a $3d^4$ configuration. CN$^-$ is a strong field ligand. The complex is octahedral and low spin ($t_{2g}^4 e_g^0$) with $2$ unpaired electrons. (Paramagnetic)

8. $[\text{Ti(CN)}_6]^{3-}$: Ti$^{3+}$ has a $3d^1$ configuration. The complex is octahedral ($t_{2g}^1 e_g^0$) with $1$ unpaired electron. (Paramagnetic)

9. $[\text{Cu(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$: Cu$^{2+}$ has a $3d^9$ configuration. The complex is octahedral ($t_{2g}^6 e_g^3$) with $1$ unpaired electron. (Paramagnetic)

10. $[\text{Co(C}_2\text{O}_4\text{)}_3]^{3-}$: Co$^{3+}$ has a $3d^6$ configuration. Oxalate ($\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$) acts as a strong field ligand for Co$^{3+}$. The complex is octahedral and low spin ($t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$) with all electrons paired. (Diamagnetic)

Therefore, the paramagnetic complexes are $[\text{MnBr}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Mn(CN)}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Ti(CN)}_6]^{3-}$, and $[\text{Cu(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$.

The total number of paramagnetic complexes is $6$.

Answer: $6$

Complexes in which central atom has unpaired electrons is said to be paramagnetic in nature and where unpaired electrons are not present are diamagnetic in nature.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Since spin only magnetic moment is 4.90 BM so number of unpaired electrons must be 4. There are two possibilities:

(1) If the complex is octahedral, then it has to be high spin complex with configuration t2g2,1,1eg1,1, in that case CFSE=4×-0.4Δ0+2×0.6Δ0=-0.4Δ0
(2) If the complex is tetrahedral then its electronic configuration will be =egt2g2,11,1,1 and CFSE will be=3X -0.6Δt+3X0.4Δt=-0.6Δt

subject: Chemistry
solution:
With weak field ligands Mn(II) will be of high spin and with strong field ligands it will be of low spin. Ni(II) tetrahedral complexes will be genrally of high spin due to sp3 hybridisation. It can very rarely be of low spin when ligand field is very strong.

Mn(II) is of light pink color in aqueous solution.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Aluminium Chloride in acidified aqueous solution forms octahedral Al(H2O)63+ ion.

AlCl3→Acidified aqueous solutionAlH2O63+

In this complex the 3d orbital of Al are involved and the hybridisation state of Al is sp3d2.

Hence, the answer is option A.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
MnBr42- is a tetrahedral complex. The electronic configuration Mn2+ise2t23 and it has 5 unpaired electrons.

∴μ=5(5+2)=35 B. M.

CoF63- is an octahedral complex and it is high spin complex. The electronic configuration of Co3+ is  t2g4eg2, it has 4 unpaired electrons

∴μ=4(4+2)=24 B.M. 

Mn(CN)63- is an octahedral complex and it is low spin complex. The electronic configuration Mn3+ is t2g4eg0 it has two unpaired electrons

∴μ=2(2+2)=8 B.M 

[Fe(CN)6]3- is an octahedral complex and it is low spin complex. The electronic configuration of Fe3+ is t2g5eg0 and it has one unpaired electron.

∴μ=1(1+2)=23 B.M 

Hence the correct answer is option 3
